sentences.text: We didn't know what kind of treatment-- I mean, 'treatment' is always in quotation marks, when it comes to a concentration camp.
And I don't remember what they gave me, or how they treated me.
All I remember that after a number of days, it was still dark outside.
I woke up.
And I felt something very heavy pressing on my right shoulder.
I couldn't figure out, because there was no lamp, no light, no nothing in the block, in the area where I was.
But then gradually, as dawn was breaking, I realized that the middle-aged lady next to me died.
And probably, in her last moments of life, ended up on me.
So I tried to disengage myself as gently as I could.
It was a terrible experience.
At the same moment, I saw Mother emerging from the door, near the door.
I was on the upper level of a cot-- or bunk, or whatever you call it-- and Mother was approaching me and sort of trying to help me down.

And she said, my dear child, I'll take you back to the block, because Valerie-- she's Valerie Lovy, L-O-V-Y, she's living in Israel now-- told me that after a few days of-- she just found out, the friend, Valerie, from the same town where we came from-- that she just found out that the sick are being picked up after a few days at the revier, taken away, and then nobody ever heard anything about them or from them.
I was terribly weak.
I don't know whether I mentioned it, I had some kind of dysentery.
What was called in Auschwitz the Ukrainian.
I don't know why, maybe that's how they called it on the Russian front, I don't know.
But it was just terrible.
I was so weak I couldn't stand on my feet.
Somehow, by sheer luck, during the [GERMAN], the head counts that we had to endure, a number of times for hours and hours at no end.
It was really a torture.
They counted the prisoners.
Somehow we were standing very, very close to each other so that I don't fall to the ground, which I did, before.
There were five in each, how do you say that?
You were five like this, and then I believe you were 10 like that.
So it was very easy for them to count.
But they always had some excuse to torture us with even more.
And of course, everybody was always afraid that now there will be a selection and people would be taken out.
We didn't know why or where or what are they doing this for.
We were always trying stay together, the three of us.
Mother, her sister, and I.
So somehow I eventually got better.
But then Mother got sick.
Without proper food, the horrendous heat during the day-- I say horrendous because there was no shade.
There were no flowers, there were no birds, there was nothing.
Only pansies, planted on the two sides of the steps leading down to the disrobing and gas chambers.
There were no flowers in Auschwitz, except those.
And of course, it was chilly at night.
So Mother developed, on her leg, some kind of ulceration.
I don't know what it was.
There was no cure for it there.
She didn't dare to go to the revier with it, because a bandage in Auschwitz, and the person was taken away.

So what we did was that we ripped off part of our undershirt, which was strictly forbidden, and put it around her leg during the day, when there were no [GERMAN] When the [GERMAN] came, then it was torn off, hidden somewhere, wherever we could.
And hoping for the best, that Mengele or the person who is doing the selection-- because there were selections and [GERMAN] all the time-- will not notice it.
And then she developed some kind of a boil under her arm.
And it was getting from bad to worse.
And it looked just horrendous and painful.
And the selection, and the [GERMAN] and time was going.
I mean, as time progressed it was getting worse and worse.
And we really didn't know what to do.
And then one day, following a very long selection, and this was probably-- I have to say another thing about my mother.
When she came to pick me up at the revier, happened to be on the 20th of July, 1944.
And as we learned later, that was the day of the botched assassination attempt by Count Stauffenberg and his partners against Hitler.
So Mother, before dawn, started on her journey.
She had her own mission.
And sneaking her way as gingerly as she could from her block to the revier, and hoping for the best that she's not caught by the guard.
Because if anybody left the latrine area at night, that person could be shot on the spot.
I mean, there were no questions in Auschwitz.
Only shootings, or people being taken away.
Well, as a matter of fact, she was stopped by the guard, who happened to be a regular army man.
I'm not saying that the army is innocent, or was innocent.
I'm not saying that.
But this particular person did not harm Mother.
He didn't ask questions.
He only told her that [SPEAKING GERMAN], which meant, in German, that the dog is not dead.
And he meant Hitler.
So Mother was able to fetch me and take me back to our barrack.
So she not only gave me life, Mother, but she literally saved my life in Auschwitz on that particular day.
Did your mother know what it meant when the guard told her that?
She couldn't have cared-- oh, you mean about Hitler?
Yes.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
She didn't know whether it's an assassination attempt or whatever, but that something is afoot.
20Th.
And then eventually, before the last and greatest selection, one evening there was great commotion across the way at the gypsy camp.
Trucks arrived.
Glaring floodlights.
Dogs.
For years, I couldn't look at a German shepherd, any German shepherd, because I associated in my mind the German shepherd with the Auschwitz guard dogs.
And dogs are my favorite animals.
For example, for years I couldn't-- still I cannot look in a series of freight cars without associating the cars with our convoy.
And it goes on and on.
The nightmares, the recurring-- it's not even reoccurring, I live with them.
They are always there.
I just learned to live with them.
I accepted them.
So did my mother and my aunt.
Otherwise you can't live with it.
And you have to talk about it.
But I don't talk about it unless I'm asked.
Because I believe that, with all due respect, the people are up here with the Holocaust.
Absolutely nothing.
(WHISPERING) Absolutely nothing.
I have to say something about what I started to tell about the Gypsies.
So trucks arrived, great commotion, dogs.
Glaring floodlights.
And then we saw people-- I mean, you couldn't make out the faces, but the people-- naked, being pushed and herded up on trucks and taken away.
And then, a while later, just a heavy smoke.
That was all what was left of their lives.
That was the gypsy camp.
Must have been probably the end of July.
Probably the end of July, because I believe that the very first few days in August was the time when they took us away from Auschwitz.
No.
Nothing.
Oh, no.
Nothing.
Nothing, absolutely nothing.
When first I saw red signs, I figured, oh, that must be a movie house in The Netherlands.
I mean, we have lost the logical perception of everything around us.
Totally disoriented.
Not even confused, disoriented.
We didn't know what was going on.
All we knew that we are in the middle of evil and some evil people are running it.
And the camp was run by Jews.
The kapos were Jewish, the Blockaltester was Jewish, the Lageraltester was Jewish.
And they were collaborators.
At the same time, I have to say that I was never put into the position where they were found in themselves.
But couldn't there have been a way not to do it?
I don't know.
And I'm still thinking about it.
But they did horrendous things.
Did you witness, personally, any punishments, or--
One of the Lagerkapos was Ria, R-I-A.
A tall, blond, like, heavyset woman.
Somebody told us she used to work in Hungary.
They always spoke only in German to us.
The other kapo was a short Czechoslovak woman by the name of Cili, C-I-L-I, with black hair, a little chubby on the side.
Her favorite-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I shouldn't say amusement.

But her favorite pastime was to have the prisoner-- and it was a women camp, only women, the men were in a different camp-- kneel on the pebbles-- like pebbles like this, that was separating the two rows of barracks in the camp-- holding two pieces of heavy brick like this, and kneeling and holding it until they collapsed.
Yeah, for nothing.
Or for-- I mean, what can you do wrong in Auschwitz?
Everything, and nothing.
I cannot recall that she personally beat up somebody with her two hands, or the other woman, Ria.
I don't remember ever having seen it.
They probably sort of, somehow like this.
I don't recall any physical abuse by their own hands.
I might be wrong, again.
But I don't remember.
Then there was a Rapportschreibereien who came.
Her name was-- the kapo was Rita, R-I-T-A.
And the Rapportschreibereien was Ria, R-I-A.
Rapportschreibereien, they said, was the woman who drew up the lists of how many prisoners, something like this.
Something to do with administration.
Of course, well-dressed and very clean and all that.
And then, once in a while, we saw Mengele coming and followed by a woman.
A young woman in a white smock, carrying a huge, like, a case for sketches, drawings.

And the latrine news-- because that's where you picked up the rumor from the various people-- had it that she is a Jewish Polish prisoner by the name of Hannah Lazarovich and allegedly Mengele's assistant, who is doing the drawings on his research.
Some research, as it turned out later.
And that allegedly she is his mistress.
I never read about this, I never heard it, I don't know.
But that's that.
We were in Auschwitz from the 15th of June till the very beginning of August, 1944.
Then there was a very extensive selection.
And all we were trying was to stay together, Mother, her sister and I.
And fortunately, we managed to do that.
And then they gave us a new dress-- I'm pushing it, a dress.
It had a long strap in front, and in the back that was called [GERMAN], which meant escape strip.
So it's probably phosphorescent, something like this, so when the person escapes then he is being caught and all that.
And they gave us a coat.
Of course they had everything what people needed.
Because they murdered people by the millions.
And the there was a special place, it's called Canada, where they kept all the clothing of the murdered people.
And, for example, our clothing as well, because we had to leave everything in the freight car.
We got that, and they gave us, I think a bowl or something, earlier, for the food in Auschwitz.
And we had to wait, without food or water, for most of the day and the night until they packed us in the train for the journey to the slave labor camp.
Just-- yes?
Sorry, just one question.
You never mentioned anything about a tattoo at Auschwitz.
Quite strangely, when we were shorn of all the hair we had, we never got tattooed.
Never, ever.
But on the clothing that they gave us, there was a number.
And mine was 9548, how could I forget?
Never tattooed.
We arrived in Parschnitz.
I believe it took three days.
No, nothing, absolutely nothing, nothing.
And you couldn't look out because everything was boarded.
And tiny little bit of air, even over that little opening, or slot, or whatever you want to call it.
The barbed wire, so you couldn't figure out.
We had an idea that we are somewhere up in the north.
But that was all.
Whether it was Poland or Czechoslovakia or Germany, we didn't know.
We didn't know where Auschwitz was.
You are going to work.
That's what they said.
And we arrived in Parschnitz.
And they put this up in-- it used to be probably a spinning factory or something to do with textiles.

Because when you walked around, like when you went to the bathroom-- I mean, I'm again pushing it, because it was not a bathroom, it was a closed latrine, that's what it was-- you could see some machinery, that people told us, in the know, that they resemble the knitting factory machines.
They put us up in bunks.
The lower bunk was shared by Mother and her sister.
And the upper bunk I shared with a woman who got married to somebody from our town.
And from Parschnitz-- that was Czechoslovakia, German-occupied Czechoslovakia-- to a town about two kilometers far from there to Parschnitz, where we worked at heavy machinery at the AEG factory.
It was a munitions factory.
Trautenau.
T-R-A-U-T-E-N-A-U, Trautenau.
And this went on from August of '44 until liberation.
But then, of course worked.
And the work stopped before liberation.
Mother eventually got well in Parschnitz.
There was a revier there, too, and just a little care.
Mother's wounds healed, and luckily, she made it.
But then my aunt got very sick.
It was the same type of boil.
Whether it was lack of vitamin or food or both, we don't know.
But the condition was mistreated by one of the nurses at the revier who didn't know anything about medical things.
And we were very afraid of blood poisoning, because her arm, starting from here, started getting discoloration and black.
But luckily, there was a registered nurse, a professional nurse by the name of Kaeta.
The other girl's name was Rena, R-E-N-A.
And Kaeta treated Auntie.
They performed some kind of a surgery-- of course, without anesthesia, without anything, without absolutely nothing.
Eventually, she got well, too.
And all the while, we managed not to get too sick, not to get too weak.
Because time to time, a car or truck or whatever came and picked up the sick in order to transfer them to a hospital in Gross-Rosen.
Today we know what Gross-Rosen was.
I don't know whether they had a gas chamber, but they had the crematorium, and I'm sure they had the means to put people away.
That's what happened to the sick in Parschnitz.
Well, it was heavy work.
And we were manufacturing, and then later dismantling, sea mines.
Like what you-- yes, anti-ship sea mines, that's what's they called them.
And some of us were running pressing machines, or drilling machines.
Heavy work. heavy industrial work.
We wound up in different working units, Mother, her sister and I.
But for a while, we were all working during the day.
And then they changed my schedule and I had to work the night shift.
So we were not be able to see each other.
Only to say hello on my way back in the morning and on their way to Trautenau on the road.
We couldn't see each other except over the weekend or the free day.
One free day, I believe we had, when we were not working.
I think we had one day only.
I'm not sure.
And then we went by train, under heavy SS woman's guard.
The living conditions compared to Auschwitz?
We had a bunk with a straw something, a straw pillow.
And lousy, I mean literally lousy.
I mean, we were teeming with lice, and various other vermin.
And no sanitation and no nothing.
In Auschwitz, women stopped menstruating.
That was also another thing, of course, which, after liberation, caused a lot of problems for many of us.
The total imbalance of the hormonal life and all that.
But I mean, compared to what happened to the family and the 6 million, that was nothing, absolutely nothing.
The life compared to Auschwitz was better in a way.
Because as time went by, we started hearing cannon fire.
We started seeing large units of military.
And we were hoping that at some point the Americans will come.
There was a partisan unit in the neighborhood.
And the way we found this out was that a man came who said he's a plumber, came to fix some piping when there was need to do it in the camp.
And he told us that the Russians are advancing.
And also we worked at the AEG in Trautenau with foreign slave laborers.
Among them was French people.
And one of them, by the name of George Alardet, A-L-A-R-D-E-T, told us in his broken-- he was so charming, I can hear his voice.
When he started telling us, [SPEAKING FRENCH].
Meaning there are many Russians in Poland.
So we had an idea that the Russians are advancing, but we didn't know how far they are from here, and how far the closest cannon fire is coming from.
And then sometime, I believe in January or February, there was a big commotion downstairs.
And we saw, haggard, a number of people filing in.
And then after that a lot of men coming in from the outside.
Nobody was permitted to go to the window.
And somehow we found out that these people are put on a death march, because they are moving them away from a camp-- we didn't know where that camp was-- from the advancing Russians.
And they have to march more to the west, because the Russians were moving in from the east.
And I just had to go to the window and just look, because I saw that-- I was hoping, I probably wasn't sure, that I saw one of my uncles.
And I went to the window and looked down.
And then the kitchen chef, who was called [GERMAN] came up, yelling and screaming, with a stick, who dared to go to the window?
So I stepped forward.
And then she started beating me up.
And then pushed me to the ground, and I had lacerations and bleeding and wounds and all that.
Unfortunately, it wasn't my uncle, neither one.
